Task 2¶

Collect all the paper info on the Stack Overflow dataset (you can get a list from here: https://meta.stackexchange.com/questions/134495/academic-papers-using-stack-exchange-data). The title would be fine that you can scrape. Clusters the papers (find a way to group similar paper together) and present the result on a webpage.

To solve this problem, I need to scrape the data first. I used BeautifulSoup library to scrape the data and used Kmeans algorithm for clustering the data.¶

Import Libraries¶

In [1]:
import requests
import pandas as pd
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import numpy as np 
import nltk
import pandas as pd 
import regex as re
import string
from nltk.corpus import stopwords
from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer
from wordcloud import WordCloud
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
import seaborn as sns

Data Collection¶

At first, we will scrape the data. The following function will return the paper information as a DataFrame.

In [2]:
# This function will scrape all of the paper's information.
# Return: It will return a dataframe of three columns.
# Each row contains the information of each paper.
# First column contains the author's information of paper.
# Second column contains the paper title.
# Third column contains the proceddings informations.
def get_papers_info():
    page = requests.get("https://meta.stackexchange.com/questions/134495/academic-papers-using-stack-exchange-data")
    soup = BeautifulSoup(page.content, 'html.parser')
    # Parse all answers from the page
    answers = soup.find(id='answers')
    # Find all <li> tag from each of the answers
    paper_lists = answers.find_all('li')
    # Remove all <a> tag from the <li> 
    for paper in paper_lists:
        a = paper.find_all('a')
        for x in a:
            x.extract()
    # Remove <li> tag from each element
    final_refined_data = [li.find('p') for li in paper_lists if li.find('p')]
    # Extract authors, paper title and proceddings for each papers
    paper_titles, authors_list, proceedings = [], [], []
    full_data = []
    for p in final_refined_data:
        full_doc = p.get_text()
        paper_title = p.find('strong').get_text()
        authors_proceding = full_doc.split(paper_title)
        paper_titles.append(paper_title.replace('\n', ''))
        authors_list.append(authors_proceding[0].replace('\n', ''))
        proceedings.append(authors_proceding[1].replace('\n', '').strip('.').strip(','))
    # Convert the result into DataFrame.
    df = pd.DataFrame({'authors': authors_list, 'title': paper_titles, 'proceedings': proceedings})
    return df
In [3]:
df = get_papers_info()

Lets print the information of first 5 papers.

In [4]:
df.head(5)

Lets use the paper titles and the topics of proceedings for clustering the data.¶

Preprocess the data¶

At first, we are going to preprocess the paper titles and porceedings. Later we will use the paper title and proceedings for clustering.

After going through the data, I have found that q&a exists in the data several times. And also 'question answering' phrase exists in the data multiple times. So lets convert 'q&a' into 'question answering'. As the prepositions and articles are not significant to cluster the data, lets remove them.

In [5]:
# This function will preprocess the paper title.
# Will return the preprocessed title
def clear_title(da):
  # Convert all the character to lower case
    da = da.lower()
    # After going through the data, I have found 
    # that q&a exists in the data several time. and 
    # question answering phrase also exists in the 
    # data. So lets convert q&a to question answering
    da = da.replace('q&a', 'question Answering')
    da = da.replace('q/a', 'question Answering')
    # Remove all the characters accept alphabet and numbers.
    final_data = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9]',' ', da)
    # remove some stop words:
    final_data = ' ' + final_data + ' '
    stop = [
        ' on ',
        ' of ',
        ' the ',
        ' and ',
        ' in ',
        ' for ',
        ' to ',
        ' with ',
        ' an ',
    ]
    for st in stop:
        final_data = final_data.replace(st, '')
    return final_data.strip()

To get the proceeding's topics, we will split the proceedings by 'on' key and take the second part. For exampple, lets consider the following proceeding:

Proceedings of the 16th International Conference on Mining Software Repositories

After splitting this by ' on ', we will consider the second part as the proceedings topics:

  1. First part: Proceedings of the 16th International Conference
  2. Second part: Mining Software Repositories
In [6]:
# This function will preprocess the proceedings to extract the topics. 
def clear_proceedings(da):
    da = da.lower()
    da = da.split(' on ')
    if len(da) <= 1:
        return ''
    da = da[1].split('(')[0]
    da = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9]',' ', da)
    da = re.split(' acm | ieee | acl | icpc | springer ', da)[0]
    da = re.split(' pages | volume | vol  ', da)[0]
    da = ' ' + da + ' '
    stop = [
      ' on ',
      ' of ',
      ' the ',
      ' and ',
      ' in ',
      ' for ',
      ' to ',
      ' with ',
      ' from '
      ' an ',
    ]
    for st in stop:
        da = da.replace(st, '')
    da = da.strip()
    return da
In [7]:
df['processed_title'] = df['title'].map(clear_title)
df['topics'] = df['proceedings'].map(clear_proceedings)

Finally, we will make the final data by the concatenation of the title and topics of the proceedings.

In [8]:
df['data'] = df['processed_title'] + ' on ' + df['topics']
In [9]:
list(df['data'].head(5))

 'from askinganswering  getting more involvedstack overflow on ',

Lets, see the word's orientation across the dataset. We will use the wordcloud package for this purpose.¶

In [10]:
nltk.download('stopwords')
stops = set(stopwords.words("english"))

[nltk_data] Downloading package stopwords to /root/nltk_data...
[nltk_data]   Package stopwords is already up-to-date!
In [11]:
# this function will show the word's cloud of list of sentences
def show_word_cloud(data_list):
  data_as_string = ' '.join(data_list)
  wordcloud = WordCloud(width = 800, height = 800, 
                  background_color ='white', 
                  stopwords = stops, 
                  min_font_size = 10).generate(data_as_string) 
    
  # plot the WordCloud image                        
  plt.figure(figsize = (8, 8), facecolor = None) 
  plt.imshow(wordcloud) 
  plt.axis("off") 
  plt.tight_layout(pad = 0)
  plt.show()

Lets, see the word cloud of the data.

In [12]:
show_word_cloud(df['data'])

Clustering¶

For clustering, we are going to implement the kmeans algorithm. At first, we will extract features using TfIdfvectorizer.

Feature extraction¶

In [13]:
tfidf_vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer()
tfidf = tfidf_vectorizer.fit_transform(df['data'])

Train the kmeans clustering model¶

In [14]:
# This function will return the k clusters of the data
def kmeans(k):
    km = KMeans(
        n_clusters = k
        , init = 'k-means++'
        , n_init = 10
        , tol = 0.0001
        , n_jobs = -1
        , random_state = 1
        , algorithm = 'full'
    )
    km = km.fit(tfidf)
    return km

Find the best k for kmeans¶

As we don't know the number of cuslters(k) among the data, we need to find out the number of clusters(k) of the data. Here, we will use two metrics to assump the best k for our data.

  • The Elbow Method: Calculate the Within-Cluster-Sum of Squared Errors (WSS) for different values of k, and choose the k for which WSS becomes first starts to diminish. In the plot of WSS-versus-k, this is visible as an elbow.
  • The Silhouette Method: The silhouette value measures how similar a point is to its own cluster (cohesion) compared to other clusters (separation).

Lets run the kmeans for k = 2 to k = 10.

In [15]:
kms = []
K = range(2, 11)
for k in K:
    km = kmeans(k)
    km = km.fit(tfidf)
    kms.append(km)

Now we will calculate the data for Elbow and Silhouette metrics.

In [16]:
sum_of_squared_distances, silhouette_scores = [], []
for km in kms:
    sum_of_squared_distances.append(km.inertia_)
    labels = km.labels_
    silhouette_scores.append(silhouette_score(tfidf, labels, metric = 'euclidean'))

Now, Lets plot WSS-versus-k and silhouette_score-versus-k.

In [17]:
plt.plot(K, sum_of_squared_distances, 'bx-')
plt.xlabel('k')
plt.ylabel('Sum_of_squared_distances')
plt.title('Elbow Method For Optimal k')
plt.show()

plt.plot(K, silhouette_scores, 'bx-')  
plt.xlabel('k')
plt.ylabel('Silhoette score')
plt.title('Silhouette Method For Optimal k')
plt.show()

Now, we can take a deeper look at each cluster and choose the best k. Though the graphs have lot of fluctuations, we can select 4, 6 or 9 as k for the custering. Lets select 6 as the total number of clusters.

In [18]:
# Select the cluster with k = 4
num_of_clusters = 6
cluster = kms[num_of_clusters - 2] 
# Add cluster number as labels columns
df['labels'] = cluster.labels_

Result Analysis¶

So now, lets show the paper titles of each cluster.

In [20]:
for i in range(num_of_clusters):
    cluster_i = df[df['labels'] == i]
    print('Papers of cluster: {}'.format(i + 1))
    for j, paper in enumerate(cluster_i.values):
        print('Cluster {}, Paper {} => {}{}, {}\n'.format(i + 1, j, paper[0], paper[1], paper[2]))
    print('........................................')

Lets see the word cloud of the clusters

In [21]:
for i in range(num_of_clusters):
    cluster_i = df[df['labels'] == i]
    print('Word cloud of cluster: {}'.format(i + 1))
    sentences = list(cluster_i['title'])
    show_word_cloud(sentences)

Word cloud of cluster: 1

Word cloud of cluster: 2

Word cloud of cluster: 3

Word cloud of cluster: 4

Word cloud of cluster: 5

Word cloud of cluster: 6

Lets, draw a simple histogram for each cluster to show the most dominant words from each clusters.

In [22]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
for i in range(num_of_clusters):
    cluster_i = np.array(df['labels'].to_numpy())
    idx = np.where(cluster_i == i)
    x_means = np.mean(tfidf[idx].toarray(), axis = 0)
    sorted_means = np.argsort(x_means)[::-1][:10]
    features = tfidf_vectorizer.get_feature_names()
    best_features = [features[i] for i in sorted_means]
    features_scores = [x_means[i] for i in sorted_means]
    plt.title(("Most common words in cluster {}".format(i)), fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    sns.barplot(x = features_scores , y = best_features, orient = 'h')
    plt.show()
In [22]:
